title: 4.5 and 4.6 practice
description: Practice what you learned in lesson 4.5 and 4.6!
topic_path: Empires and Belief Systems (200 to 1500 CE)

assessment_items.question_content:
**Which statement best captures the nature of religion across the Roman Empire?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: The Roman Empire allowed many local religions to flourish, often blending them with Roman beliefs.
Roman religion was strictly controlled by the emperor, who banned any foreign gods or rituals.
All citizens were required to follow a single Roman religion based on Greek mythology.
The Roman Empire had no organized religion and left religious practices up to individual households.

assessment_items.question_content:
**Which of the following best explains how trade occurred between the Roman Empire and Han Dynasty China?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: Goods were passed along a network of traders rather than by direct contact between the two empires.
Roman and Chinese merchants sailed directly between ports on the Mediterranean and the South China Sea.
The Roman Empire conquered Central Asia to control trade with China.
Trade was impossible between the two empires due to natural barriers like deserts and mountains.

assessment_items.question_content:
**What statement best explains women’s roles in Roman society?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: While technically citizens, women had limited freedom and lacked many of the rights given to men.
Women made large strides in Roman society by often serving as advisors to their sons and husbands.
Women had financial freedom but restricted political freedom as they could not vote.
Women had no freedom in Roman society, with no right to divorce or work outside the household.

assessment_items.question_content:
**How did the Han belief in the Mandate of Heaven differ from the Roman idea that emperors were divine or descended from gods?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: The Mandate of Heaven was based on moral behavior and could be lost, while Roman emperors claimed a permanent divine status.
The Mandate of Heaven gave Han emperors complete religious authority, unlike Roman rulers who shared that power with priests.
Even though both were used to justify rule, only Chinese emperors could depend on the Mandate of Heaven to stay in power.
Roman emperors ruled with limited power due to Senate control, while Han emperors ruled as gods on Earth.

assessment_items.question_content:
**Which of the following best describes the lasting legacy of the Han Dynasty in China?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: The Han Dynasty developed advanced agriculture and expanded trade networks that influenced China for centuries.
The Han Dynasty rejected Confucianism, relied heavily on slave labor, and limited foreign trade to protect the empire.
The Han Dynasty weakened central control by dividing China into independent regions and discouraged cultural unity.
The Han Dynasty focused mainly on military conquest and neglected the economy and culture, causing rapid decline.

assessment_items.question_content:
**What part of Han society or belief system helped ensure steady food supplies and economic stability?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: The Confucian emphasis on family and farming traditions encouraged stability in rural communities.
The Han Dynasty’s focus on maritime trade shifted attention away from agriculture.
Han rulers discouraged farming in favor of industrial labor and urban expansion.
The legalist policies of the Han government forced people into farming through strict punishments.

assessment_items.question_content:
**How did Confucianism and Daoism differ in the roles they allowed women to play in Chinese society?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: Daoism gave women more active roles in religion and personal life, while Confucianism generally limited women’s power.
Confucianism gave women more political power, while Daoism gave them more influence in religious life.
Daoism restricted women’s lives in all areas, while Confucianism only limited their political roles but gave them freedom in religion.
They did not differ; both severely limited women’s freedom and kept them confined to the household.

assessment_items.question_content:
**What does the Confucian idea of the “Three Obediences” mean?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: It was a belief that women should obey their fathers, husbands, and sons at different stages of life.
It was a rule stating that children must obey their teachers, elders, and local officials.
It was a legal code requiring citizens to follow the emperor, their governors, and military leaders.
It was a guideline for officials to obey their superiors, ancestors, and the laws of nature.

assessment_items.question_content:
**How did a woman’s social class affect her role and status in Roman society?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: Wealthy women had more influence, while poorer women often worked outside the home.
Lower-class women had more freedom because they were not expected to follow elite social customs.
All Roman women, regardless of class, were expected to remain inside the home and avoid public life.
Only upper-class women were legally allowed to own property or seek a divorce.

assessment_items.question_content:
**How did women’s role in public life differ between the Roman Empire and the Han Empire?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: Han women were limited to private spaces, while Roman women could enter public spaces if accompanied by a man.
Han women frequently played a role in public life, while Roman women were confined to the household.
Han women were more likely to visit public baths, while Roman women attended religious ceremonies.
Han women could enter public life when accompanied by a man, while Roman women moved freely.

assessment_items.question_content:
**Does the statement, *“Let a woman modestly yield to others; let her respect others; let her put others first. Herself, last,”* reflect Confucian views on women during the Han Dynasty? Why or why not?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: Yes, because this statement reflects the secondary role women were expected to take under Confucianism.
Yes, because Confucianism taught respect for others as a core principle.
No, because Confucianism taught women to put themselves first in Han society.
No, because this statement suggests women had value, while Confucianism saw no use for women.

assessment_items.question_content:
>In 42 BCE, Hortensia, an elite Roman woman, proclaimed, “Why should we pay taxes when we do not share in the offices, military commands, nor, in short, the government?


**Based on Hortensia’s speech, what does her statement reveal about women’s roles in Roman society?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: Women were required to pay taxes but were excluded from holding political office or military positions.
Women had full political and military rights but chose not to participate.
Women held significant government positions but were exempt from paying taxes.
Women actively commanded armies but were not allowed to vote in elections.

assessment_items.question_content:
**What makes it more challenging to understand the experiences of poor women than rich women in ancient Rome and Han China?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: Poor women left fewer written records and were less often mentioned in historical documents.
Poor women were legally forbidden from participating in public life, so they were invisible to historians.
Rich women purposely hid the experiences of poor women to maintain social hierarchy.
Poor women were nomadic and moved frequently, making it difficult to track their lives.
